content.text: Every Maven project has exactly one central configuration file — the

— that governs how the project is built, what it depends on, and how it is packaged and deployed. Standing for Project Object Model, the POM is both the identity card and the build blueprint of a Maven project. Understanding its structure and lifecycle is the prerequisite for any serious work with Maven or Spring Boot.

The POM is an XML document that describes a Maven project at every level Maven cares about: its coordinates (the unique identity in the repository world), its dependencies, the plugins that build it, resource filtering rules, test configuration, deployment targets, and environmental profiles. Maven reads the POM before executing any goal and uses it as the complete specification of "what is this project and how should it be processed."
The POM is also a tree. A child project's POM inherits from a parent POM, which may itself inherit from a grandparent, up to Maven's built-in
that defines sensible defaults for all projects (source directories, the Maven Central repository, plugin versions, etc.). The union of a project's own POM with all its ancestor POMs is called the
, and it is this effective POM that Maven actually acts on when building.
At a minimum every POM must declare:
. Everything else is inherited from parents or the Super POM.
Before tools like Maven, building a Java project meant hand-crafting
or Ant scripts — imperative procedures that described
the project is. Every team reinvented the same compile-then-test-then-package sequence. Dependencies were jars committed into the repository or copied manually between machines.

The POM solves these pain points through a declarative model: you state what the project is and what it needs; Maven figures out the procedure. The build lifecycle — validate → compile → test → package → verify → install → deploy — is baked in. The POM's role is to supply the specifics (which version of Spring, which Java release, which test runner arguments) so Maven can execute the standard lifecycle correctly.
For Spring and Spring Boot projects specifically, the POM is where you attach to the
bill-of-materials, declare starters, and control the fat-jar packaging plugin. A single line in the POM can transform a plain Java class into a self-contained, deployable application.
Build configuration, dependency versions, plugin settings, and deployment coordinates all live in one file. No hidden build scripts scattered across the repository.
against the same POM will produce an identical artifact, because Maven resolves exact versions from remote repositories rather than using whatever jars happen to be on the classpath.
A parent POM can declare shared versions, plugin configuration, and quality gates once; dozens of child modules inherit automatically without duplication.
Every major Java library, framework, and toolchain (Checkstyle, SpotBugs, JaCoCo, Nexus, Jenkins) speaks Maven. If a tool supports Java, it almost certainly has a Maven plugin.
Maven fetches not just the declared dependency but its dependencies' dependencies, applying a nearest-wins conflict resolution strategy automatically.
The Super POM defaults establish that source lives in
. Every Maven developer understands the layout immediately.
Even minimal configuration requires dozens of lines of XML. Gradle's Groovy/Kotlin DSL can express the same intent in a fraction of the text.

Maven's build lifecycle is fixed and opinionated. Unusual build steps (code generation, multiple packaging stages) require plugin configuration that fights against the model rather than extending it naturally.
Dependency conflict resolution is automatic but not always correct.
Nearest-wins can select an incompatible version of a transitive dependency, causing
at runtime. Debugging these requires reading the dependency tree with
Large inheritance chains make it hard to understand where a particular version or configuration comes from. The effective POM is a required escape hatch:
Maven's single-threaded lifecycle (unless configured otherwise) and lack of incremental compilation at the source level makes it slower than Gradle for large monorepos.
When Maven starts, it reads the POM and builds the Effective POM by merging: the project's own
→ the Super POM (embedded inside Maven itself). Child declarations win over parent declarations; the Super POM provides the safety net for everything not declared.
Maven then executes the requested lifecycle. Each lifecycle (default, clean, site) is composed of phases. The default lifecycle's key phases in order are:
— confirm the POM is structurally correct.
— bundle compiled classes into a JAR, WAR, or other artifact.
— run integration tests and quality checks.
— install the artifact into the local Maven repository (
), making it available to other local projects.
— upload the artifact to a remote repository (Nexus, Artifactory).
runs every phase up to and including
— that is Maven's key convention: a lifecycle phase implies all earlier phases.
Maven fetches dependencies from repositories (local cache first, then Central or any configured remote) and stores them in
. For a dependency declared at version A that transitively requires version B of a common library, while another direct dependency requires version C, Maven applies
: the dependency closest to the root of the dependency graph (fewest hops) wins. A declaration directly in the project's own POM always wins over transitive.
Scopes control which classpath a dependency participates in and whether it is included in the packaged artifact:
The diagram below maps the main sections of a
file and what each section controls. In practice a given project's POM usually contains only the sections it needs; most of the rest is inherited from the parent.

attrs.alt: Maven POM structure showing all major sections: project coordinates, parent, properties, modules, dependencies, dependencyManagement, build, profiles, reporting, distributionManagement, and repositories

content.text: section is the project's identity in the Maven universe. The
only centralises versions without actually downloading anything. The
section configures the compiler, packaging plugin, and resources.
overlay any section for environment-specific overrides.
Think of the POM as a

for the project, where the card can inherit sections from a master template (the parent POM). The recipe says: "these are my ingredients (dependencies), here is the equipment I need (plugins), and this is the form I want the finished dish in (packaging)."
section is the pantry stock list — it says "we buy brand X of tomatoes" but does not actually put tomatoes in any dish. The
section says "this dish uses tomatoes," and because the pantry stock list already pins the brand (version), the recipe card for this dish only needs to say the ingredient name.
The POM is used whenever a project is built with Maven — there is no opt-out. Within this constraint, different sections of the POM have specific usage patterns:
when starting a Spring Boot project.
gives you pre-configured compiler settings, sensible plugin defaults, and a curated bill-of-materials for all Spring libraries.
If your organisation has a company-wide parent POM, pin shared library versions there so all child projects get consistent versions without repeating them.
When a logical system (API + core + web) benefits from being built together but deployed separately, a parent POM with
at the root build everything in dependency order.
Different databases, different logging levels, or different resource filters between dev and prod are cleanly expressed as profiles rather than as separate build scripts.
Do not use the POM for application configuration.
Runtime settings (server port, database URL, feature flags) belong in
or environment variables, not in the POM.
How to Implement — Creating and Structuring a POM

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0
             https://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">

  <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

  <!-- GAV coordinates: the project's unique identity in the Maven universe -->
  <groupId>com.example</groupId>
  <artifactId>hello-maven</artifactId>
  <version>1.0.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
  <!-- packaging defaults to "jar" if omitted -->
  <packaging>jar</packaging>

  <name>Hello Maven</name>
  <description>Minimal Maven project demonstrating POM structure</description>

  <properties>
    <!-- Java release used by the compiler plugin -->
    <java.version>21</java.version>
    <maven.compiler.source>${java.version}</maven.compiler.source>
    <maven.compiler.target>${java.version}</maven.compiler.target>
    <project.build.sourceEncoding>UTF-8</project.build.sourceEncoding>
  </properties>

  <dependencies>
    <!-- JUnit 5 — test-scoped, never bundled in the jar -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.junit.jupiter</groupId>
      <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
      <version>5.10.2</version>
      <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>

  <build>
    <plugins>
      <!-- Surefire 3.x required to run JUnit 5 tests -->
      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
        <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
        <version>3.2.5</version>
      </plugin>
    </plugins>
  </build>

</project>
Spring Boot POM with Starter Parent

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0"
         xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0
             https://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">

  <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

  <!-- Inherit Spring Boot's opinionated defaults -->
  <parent>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
    <version>3.3.1</version>
    <!-- empty relativePath = resolve from Maven Central, not local filesystem -->
    <relativePath/>
  </parent>

  <groupId>com.example</groupId>
  <artifactId>product-service</artifactId>
  <version>0.0.1-SNAPSHOT</version>

  <properties>
    <!-- The parent already configures maven.compiler.source/target;
         override only if you need a different Java release. -->
    <java.version>21</java.version>
  </properties>

  <dependencies>
    <!-- Web starter: brings in Spring MVC, Tomcat, Jackson -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
      <!-- No <version> — the parent's BOM manages it -->
    </dependency>

    <!-- JPA + Hibernate -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-jpa</artifactId>
    </dependency>

    <!-- H2 for dev/test; runtime scope so it is not on the compile classpath -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>com.h2database</groupId>
      <artifactId>h2</artifactId>
      <scope>runtime</scope>
    </dependency>

    <!-- Spring Boot test infrastructure (JUnit 5, Mockito, etc.) -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>
      <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>

  <build>
    <plugins>
      <!-- Repackage into an executable fat jar / fat war -->
      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
      </plugin>
    </plugins>
  </build>

</project>
in the parent prevents the classic problem of different child modules importing different versions of the same library:

<!-- In the company parent POM -->
<dependencyManagement>
  <dependencies>

    <!-- Import the Spring Boot BOM to align all Spring library versions -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-dependencies</artifactId>
      <version>3.3.1</version>
      <type>pom</type>
      <scope>import</scope>
    </dependency>

    <!-- Pin a custom internal library version once for all child modules -->
    <dependency>
      <groupId>com.acme</groupId>
      <artifactId>acme-commons</artifactId>
      <version>2.7.0</version>
    </dependency>

  </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>

<!-- In any child module — no version needed -->
<dependencies>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
    <!-- version resolved from BOM above -->
  </dependency>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>com.acme</groupId>
    <artifactId>acme-commons</artifactId>
    <!-- version resolved from parent's dependencyManagement -->
  </dependency>
</dependencies>

<profiles>

  <!-- Active by default for local development -->
  <profile>
    <id>dev</id>
    <activation>
      <activeByDefault>true</activeByDefault>
    </activation>
    <properties>
      <spring.profiles.active>dev</spring.profiles.active>
      <skip.integration.tests>true</skip.integration.tests>
    </properties>
    <dependencies>
      <!-- H2 in-memory DB only in dev -->
      <dependency>
        <groupId>com.h2database</groupId>
        <artifactId>h2</artifactId>
        <scope>runtime</scope>
      </dependency>
    </dependencies>
  </profile>

  <!-- Activated explicitly on CI: mvn verify -P ci -->
  <profile>
    <id>ci</id>
    <properties>
      <spring.profiles.active>ci</spring.profiles.active>
      <skip.integration.tests>false</skip.integration.tests>
    </properties>
    <dependencies>
      <!-- Real PostgreSQL driver in CI -->
      <dependency>
        <groupId>org.postgresql</groupId>
        <artifactId>postgresql</artifactId>
        <scope>runtime</scope>
      </dependency>
    </dependencies>
  </profile>

</profiles>
Real-World Example — Multi-Module Spring Boot Monorepo

A typical enterprise Spring Boot system consists of separate Maven modules for the domain core, the REST API layer, and the batch processing tier. The root POM declares the reactor (build order) and version management; each child POM inherits it.

<!-- Root POM — packaging must be "pom" for multi-module projects -->
<project>
  <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

  <parent>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
    <version>3.3.1</version>
    <relativePath/>
  </parent>

  <groupId>com.acme.platform</groupId>
  <artifactId>platform</artifactId>
  <version>4.2.0</version>
  <packaging>pom</packaging>

  <!-- Maven builds modules in dependency order automatically -->
  <modules>
    <module>platform-core</module>      <!-- shared domain model, no Spring -->
    <module>platform-api</module>       <!-- Spring MVC REST endpoints -->
    <module>platform-batch</module>     <!-- Spring Batch ETL jobs -->
    <module>platform-integration-tests</module>
  </modules>

  <properties>
    <java.version>21</java.version>
    <!-- Pin shared non-Boot library versions here -->
    <mapstruct.version>1.5.5.Final</mapstruct.version>
    <archunit.version>1.3.0</archunit.version>
  </properties>

  <dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
      <!-- Inter-module dependencies managed centrally -->
      <dependency>
        <groupId>com.acme.platform</groupId>
        <artifactId>platform-core</artifactId>
        <version>${project.version}</version>
      </dependency>
      <dependency>
        <groupId>org.mapstruct</groupId>
        <artifactId>mapstruct</artifactId>
        <version>${mapstruct.version}</version>
      </dependency>
    </dependencies>
  </dependencyManagement>

  <build>
    <pluginManagement>
      <!-- Configure the Spring Boot repackaging plugin once for all children -->
      <plugins>
        <plugin>
          <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
          <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
          <configuration>
            <!-- Exclude Lombok from fat jar -->
            <excludes>
              <exclude>
                <groupId>org.projectlombok</groupId>
                <artifactId>lombok</artifactId>
              </exclude>
            </excludes>
          </configuration>
        </plugin>
      </plugins>
    </pluginManagement>
  </build>

</project>
Each child POM then simply references what it needs. The
child POM needs only the Spring Web starter and the core module:

<!-- platform-api/pom.xml -->
<project>
  <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

  <parent>
    <groupId>com.acme.platform</groupId>
    <artifactId>platform</artifactId>
    <version>4.2.0</version>
  </parent>

  <artifactId>platform-api</artifactId>
  <!-- groupId and version inherited from parent -->

  <dependencies>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>com.acme.platform</groupId>
      <artifactId>platform-core</artifactId>
      <!-- version managed in root dependencyManagement -->
    </dependency>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
    </dependency>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>
      <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>

  <build>
    <plugins>
      <!-- spring-boot-maven-plugin config is inherited from root pluginManagement -->
      <plugin>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
      </plugin>
    </plugins>
  </build>

</project>
Putting version in <dependencies> when it should be in <dependencyManagement>

<!-- ❌ Version scattered in each module's <dependencies> -->
<!-- module-a/pom.xml -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
  <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
  <version>2.17.1</version>  <!-- repeated in every module -->
</dependency>

<!-- ✅ Pin version once in the parent's dependencyManagement -->
<!-- parent/pom.xml -->
<dependencyManagement>
  <dependencies>
    <dependency>
      <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
      <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
      <version>2.17.1</version>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>

<!-- module-a/pom.xml — no version -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
  <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
</dependency>
Forgetting to set packaging="pom" on the reactor/parent

<!-- ❌ Parent POM with <modules> but wrong packaging -->
<packaging>jar</packaging>  <!-- Maven will try to build a jar and fail -->

<!-- ✅ Multi-module parent must use pom packaging -->
<packaging>pom</packaging>
Using compile scope for Servlet API / Jakarta EE APIs

<!-- ❌ Will bundle jakarta.servlet into the WAR alongside the container's copy -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>jakarta.servlet</groupId>
  <artifactId>jakarta.servlet-api</artifactId>
  <version>6.0.0</version>
  <!-- missing scope -->
</dependency>

<!-- ✅ The servlet container (Tomcat, Jetty) provides this at runtime -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>jakarta.servlet</groupId>
  <artifactId>jakarta.servlet-api</artifactId>
  <version>6.0.0</version>
  <scope>provided</scope>
</dependency>
Specifying version alongside a spring-boot-starter-parent import

<!-- ❌ Overrides the BOM's careful version alignment for that library -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
  <version>3.3.0</version>  <!-- BOM already manages this -->
</dependency>

<!-- ✅ Let the BOM manage the version -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
</dependency>
Hardcoding dependency versions as literals when a property is cleaner

<!-- ❌ Hard to find and update when upgrading -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.mapstruct</groupId>
  <artifactId>mapstruct</artifactId>
  <version>1.5.5.Final</version>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.mapstruct</groupId>
  <artifactId>mapstruct-processor</artifactId>
  <version>1.5.5.Final</version>  <!-- easy to forget to update both -->
</dependency>

<!-- ✅ Single property to update, automatically aligned -->
<properties>
  <mapstruct.version>1.5.5.Final</mapstruct.version>
</properties>
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.mapstruct</groupId>
  <artifactId>mapstruct</artifactId>
  <version>${mapstruct.version}</version>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.mapstruct</groupId>
  <artifactId>mapstruct-processor</artifactId>
  <version>${mapstruct.version}</version>
</dependency>
Use the starter parent or a company parent POM.
Never manage plugin versions individually in a greenfield project. The Spring Boot parent already pins compiler plugin, surefire, failsafe, and many others to tested-together versions.
when debugging unexpected behaviour. The effective POM shows exactly what Maven will act on, including all inherited configuration.
Audit the dependency tree before release.
to see which version of each transitive dependency was selected and why.
Keep the POM in version control but never commit credentials.
Repository passwords and deploy credentials belong in
(which is not in the repository), referenced from the POM by server id only.
) explicitly. Without this, the compiler plugin defaults to Java 1.6 source/target, causing confusing failures when you use modern language features.
Use the Maven Versions Plugin to keep dependencies current.
to update the POM automatically (review changes before committing).
The POM itself has no runtime performance impact — it is a build-time artifact. Build performance is however shaped by how the POM is structured:
to build independent modules in parallel. In a 10-module reactor this can halve wall-clock build time.
Dependency bloat increases compilation and test time.
Every additional JAR on the classpath increases class-scanning time. Audit dependencies periodically; remove unused ones.
Maven checks the remote repository on every build for
(offline mode) for faster local iteration after initial download, or switch to release versions for CI.
packages all runtime dependencies into the jar. This is convenient but produces large artifacts (often 60–120 MB). Use container layering (
inside the plugin configuration) so that slow-changing dependency layers are cached by Docker and not retransmitted on every deploy.
— prints the merged POM Maven actually executes, invaluable for debugging inheritance.
— shows the full dependency graph with conflict resolutions, essential for diagnosing version clashes.
— identifies used-but-undeclared and declared-but-unused dependencies, helping trim POM bloat.
) — automates dependency version upgrades and reports outdated libraries.
) — enforces rules: minimum Maven/Java version, banned dependencies, no snapshot in release builds. Runs in the validate phase and fails fast.
— the IDE reads the POM and provides code completion for artifact coordinates, XML schema validation, inline dependency conflict highlighting, and the Maven tool window for lifecycle goal execution.
— a shell script committed to the repository that downloads the correct Maven version automatically. Eliminates "works on my machine" issues caused by different Maven installations. Created with
Universal; every tool has a Maven plugin
Excellent; dominant in Android, growing in Spring

The POM is the entry point for supply-chain security. Every dependency declared (and its transitive tree) is a potential vulnerability surface. OWASP provides the Dependency-Check Maven plugin to scan all declared dependencies against known CVE databases:

<build>
  <plugins>
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.owasp</groupId>
      <artifactId>dependency-check-maven</artifactId>
      <version>9.2.0</version>
      <configuration>
        <!-- Fail build if any dependency has CVSS >= 7 (High) -->
        <failBuildOnCVSS>7</failBuildOnCVSS>
        <suppressionFile>owasp-suppressions.xml</suppressionFile>
      </configuration>
      <executions>
        <execution>
          <goals><goal>check</goal></goals>
        </execution>
      </executions>
    </plugin>
  </plugins>
</build>

Maven Central publishes SHA-1 checksums alongside every artifact. Maven verifies these automatically and rejects artifacts that do not match, preventing man-in-the-middle substitution attacks during download. For maximum security in corporate environments, route all downloads through an authenticated Nexus or Artifactory mirror (configured in
— not in the POM) so that the organization controls what versions are available to builds.
Never Use LATEST or RELEASE as Dependency Versions

<!-- ❌ Non-reproducible: resolves to whatever "latest" means today -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>com.example</groupId>
  <artifactId>my-lib</artifactId>
  <version>LATEST</version>
</dependency>

<!-- ✅ Pin the exact version: reproducible, auditable -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>com.example</groupId>
  <artifactId>my-lib</artifactId>
  <version>2.3.1</version>
</dependency>

<plugin>
  <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
  <artifactId>maven-enforcer-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>3.5.0</version>
  <executions>
    <execution>
      <id>enforce</id>
      <goals><goal>enforce</goal></goals>
      <configuration>
        <rules>
          <!-- Require Maven 3.9+ and Java 21+ -->
          <requireMavenVersion>
            <version>[3.9,)</version>
          </requireMavenVersion>
          <requireJavaVersion>
            <version>[21,)</version>
          </requireJavaVersion>
          <!-- Prevent snapshot dependencies in release builds -->
          <requireReleaseDeps>
            <onlyWhenRelease>true</onlyWhenRelease>
          </requireReleaseDeps>
          <!-- Fail on duplicate dependencies that differ only in version -->
          <banDuplicatePomDependencyVersions/>
        </rules>
      </configuration>
    </execution>
  </executions>
</plugin>
The POM is Maven's single source of truth
— project coordinates, dependencies, build plugins, and deployment configuration all live in one file.
Every project implicitly inherits from the Super POM
declarations add additional defaults; the Effective POM (
without repeating the version. This prevents version drift between modules.
Dependency scopes control classpath membership and packaging
, which provides a curated bill-of-materials; Spring dependency versions should not be manually overridden unless absolutely necessary.
Multi-module projects use a root POM with
entries; Maven resolves build order from inter-module dependencies automatically.

at the POM level includes pinning exact dependency versions, scanning with the OWASP Dependency-Check plugin, enforcing minimum Maven/Java versions with the Enforcer plugin, and routing downloads through an authenticated artifact proxy.
Never commit secrets to the POM
